metadata.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

Good morning. One is Will Law from Accomi. I am presenting the W3C web transport group update on behalf of my co-chair, Yannivari Brury from Mozilla. So we published a working draft back on February 4th. That's the latest one. Our charter has been extended to June 30th at this point. And we have a timetable that is quite optimistic for a release this year. I don't want to say by July 26th. That's what we're targeting right now. But we're certainly aiming for that. It's been several years work. The good news is we defined a candidate recommendation milestone for our issues. We're 100% complete on that. We've closed 90 issues. And we're looking in a really good spot at this point to get the spec out. Next slide please. So I just want to summarize some of the major updates and decisions that we've had on the W3C side of web transport since the last IETF meeting. The biggest change we've had was an integration with fetch. So for those who know the spec we were tying in at quite a low level in fetch. And it was suggested that we tie in at the highest level. This allows us to use the connect request during fetch. It allows us to inherit any future improvements in fetch. And we also got header support, which is one of the primary requested features for the browser-based version of web transport. So this work has been done now. We added some additional connection stats by it's acknowledged and application by sent to improve the statistics. And we also removed framing overhead in order to give applications a bit of estimate of exactly how much data was being sent. Next slide. We updated the explainer. It hadn't been updated since 2021. So the examples were little old. There was a lot of new material in there. So there's some really nice code that's been added. Code examples showing data-gram usage, stream usage, constructor, all the various new constructor options that we have. So I encourage people to look at the explainer. It's really up to date snapshot of where the spec is today. There's been worked on on CSP and and search verification along with a new keyword unsafe web transport hashes, which can be used. And we've also improved the headers constructor option. Sorry, in the constructor there's now an opportunity to send headers. So you can send a baratoken for example in a format that you might be familiar with on HTTP and send it down to the server. Next slide. And just the last two updates we renamed in coming max buffer data grams to be, sorry, incoming high watermarked be uncovered max buffer data grams because it was implying that there was a stream like back pressure, which there just wasn't here. We also changed it from a double to long. These are technical changes and I think we can go to next slide. So the very good news within the W3C world web transport has been accepted as a focus area for interoperability 26. So interoperability is a hardcore effort amongst the major browser vendors to pick a subset of technologies and really dive deep into interoperability. So the fact that web transport has been selected as one of these is fantastic. It means I likelihood it having a highly interoperable solution by the end of 2026 is very good. And that that boats well for all the applications, all the technologies that depend on web transport in the browser such as media over quick. So thank you to the browser vendors for taking on web transport in this format. And then last slide. So I mentioned we've closed out our candidate recommendation milestone. When I'm moving to the next step of the W3C release process, which is called wide review, we ask other experts within W3C to come and view the spec. And then we will move down the release roadmap. Our goal is to get it out in approximately August 26 this year. Are there any questions that is the end of our W3C report? I don't hear any. Thank you very much. Thank you.
